messages.text: When I was six, I made my first drawing after reading about a boa that swallowed its prey whole. I did not yet know that every adult would call my picture a hat.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: It was not a hat. It was a snake with an elephant inside. I could imagine the elephant clearly.
The adult world answered with advice about numbers, geography, grammar, and careers. So I put away my colored pencils and became an ordinary pilot.
Years later my plane broke above the Sahara desert. The engine coughed once, shook twice, and fell silent over sand that seemed endless.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: I had water for eight days, a toolbox, and no mechanic within a thousand miles. I had to repair the engine myself.
At dawn, after a night of cold stars, a sudden small voice woke me as if it had fallen from the sky.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Please, will you draw a sheep for me?

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: What are you doing here, in this desert, alone at sunrise?

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: If you please, a sheep. That is my request.
His request was so serious that I forgot thirst, fear, and the broken engine. I drew the old snake picture first.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: No. I do not want an elephant inside a snake. A sheep is smaller.
I tried one sheep, then another, but the curious child rejected each with polite firmness. One was sick, one was a ram, one was too old.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Here is a box. The sheep you want is inside it.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: That is exactly right. Do you think this sheep needs much grass?
Only a child could accept a box with such trust. Only a child could make a tired pilot in the desert feel less ordinary.
While I prepared tools for the repair, he studied the drawing and asked quiet questions, but he answered almost none of mine.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Where did you come from? Did another plane bring you here?

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: The important thing is that the sheep will be small. Where I live, everything is small.
That was how I met the little prince: not through proper introduction, but through a drawing, a broken engine, and a request impossible to refuse.
By evening the desert no longer felt empty. A strange friendship had started, and my old habit of seeing beyond the surface returned.
Before that morning, I had trusted practical things: fuel, maps, bolts, and the stubborn arithmetic of survival. The boy made those adult tools feel incomplete.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: The sheep in the box is not asleep forever, is it? It will wake when it is hungry.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Of course. A sheep in a box follows the rules of imagination better than any sheep I draw badly.
He accepted that explanation with grave satisfaction. Then he walked around the plane, curious about each wing, wheel, and dented panel.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: This thing cannot have brought you from very far. It is too heavy to belong among stars.
I laughed, then stopped, because he had guessed more than I had told him. The desert had given me a child who spoke as if distance were familiar.
I had spent years hiding the child who drew from imagination. The little prince found that hidden part faster than any adult ever had.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: When you repair the engine, do you also repair the pilot?
I noticed, too, that he never treated the drawing as a trick. To him the box was practical because it protected the sheep and protected my pride.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: That small mercy was the first repair he made in me.
